items.headline: How I Escaped My Golden Cage
items.angle: Christian tells his personal 'rock bottom' story of burnout. He was successful but trapped by his own business, chained to a laptop, and shares the specific moment he decided to rebuild everything for freedom, not just for growth.
hooks: My business was making seven figures, and I was the most miserable I'd ever been.
This is the photo that made me burn my entire business to the ground.
I had to fire myself from the company I built.
It was 3 AM and I was staring at a spreadsheet, realizing I had built myself a prison.
I missed my best friend's wedding because of a client crisis I thought I couldn't escape.
talking_points: I remember the exact moment it all broke. It was a perfect afternoon, and I got a text about a whale shark sighting just offshore. My gear was ready, but I was trapped on my laptop, stuck in a business I built to be free.
I had a huge task in front of me. I had to completely rebuild my company so it could run without me, without destroying my income in the process.
I had done everything the 'gurus' tell you. I put in the 16 hour days, I hustled, I built a successful agency. But I wasn't the owner, I was the bottleneck for every single decision.
So I was faced with a choice. I could keep grinding, make more money, and accept that my life would be lived through a screen.
Or, I could tear the whole thing down. I could let go of control, and build something new, something that used systems and automation to buy back my time.
And that's what I did. I went all in on building engines that could do 80 percent of the work for me. It was terrifying letting go of that control.
But it worked. The business kept growing, and my hours were cut in half. I started living the life I was actually working for in the first place.

I can tell you two things for sure. It would not have happened if I kept believing my personal time was the most valuable input. And it would not have happened if I didn't get obsessed with automation.
This is the whole idea behind FieldWork. If you feel trapped in the business you built to be free, comment 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you the guide on how to build your own freedom engine.

items.headline: Stop Selling the Engine. Sell the Field.
items.angle: Adapt the competitor's 'brain-side' dichotomy to our core message. Frame the wrong way as 'selling the engine' (the complex systems, the 'how') and the right way as 'selling the field' (the offline freedom, the 'why').
hooks: You are selling your business all wrong. Here is how to fix it.

talking_points: There are two ways to sell an online business, and most founders are choosing the one that chains them to their desk.
When you sell the features, the tactics, the complexity, and all the bells and whistles of your system, you are selling the engine.

This exclusively attracts people who love tinkering with engines. They will fight you on the details, get lost in the process, and never actually get the business running so they can enjoy their life.
It is why so much hard work building a great offer ends up attracting people who just want to stay busy instead of getting results.
Conversely, if you sell the field, you sell the destination. You use stories and real outcomes to show what life is like when the engine just works.
This is powerful because you are not just selling a program, you are selling a tangible change in someone's life. So you have to be able to deliver.
To do this right, first get clear on the true desired outcome. Is it a four day work week? Is it coaching clients from a sailboat? Is it just being home for dinner every night?
Then, lead with that outcome. Tell the story of what it feels like to close the laptop on a Thursday, knowing the business is running without you.
When you do this, you stop selling information and you start selling transformation. You are selling a ticket out of the office and into the world.
Use this approach with integrity, because when you sell someone a destination, you have a real responsibility to help them get there.
